children.name: Apis
children.description: Honey bees — the eusocial bees that produce and store honey in wax combs, living in perennial colonies of tens of thousands of workers. The Western Honey Bee is probably the single most economically important insect on Earth, providing both honey and wax and — more significantly — pollinating a third of global crop production. A single colony may contain 50,000–80,000 workers, all daughters of a single queen who may live for five years. The waggle dance — a figure-eight movement that communicates the direction and distance of food sources — is one of the most sophisticated non-primate communication systems known.

children.name: Apis mellifera
children.description: The world's most managed insect — kept by humans for at least 9,000 years. The waggle dance encodes the angle of the food source relative to the sun and its distance. Population declines linked to pesticides, varroa mite, habitat loss, and disease are a growing food-security concern.

children.name: Apis cerana
children.description: The original honey bee of Asia, co-evolved with its parasite Varroa destructor — which devastates Western Honey Bee colonies. Asian bees exhibit thermal balling behaviour: surrounding and vibrating around hornets to generate lethal heat (~47°C).

children.name: Apis dorsata
children.description: Builds single, exposed combs up to a metre wide suspended from cliff faces and high branches. The combs of cliff-nesting colonies in the Himalayas contain 'mad honey' — honey produced from rhododendron nectar containing grayanotoxins, with psychoactive and medicinal properties.

children.name: Apis florea
children.description: The smallest of the honey bees, native to South and Southeast Asia. Builds single open combs on branches and shrubs. Has a reduced sting that cannot penetrate human skin.

children.name: Apis andreniformis
children.description: The smallest honey bee species, found in Southeast Asia. The black dwarf honey bee nests in dense vegetation and is known for its highly defensive behaviour.

children.name: Apis nuluensis
children.description: A recently recognized honey bee species restricted to the mountainous regions of Borneo. Distinguished by its smaller size and darker colouration compared to Apis cerana.

children.name: Apis koschevnikovi
children.description: A honey bee species found in Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula. Also known as the red honey bee for its distinctive reddish-brown colour.

children.name: Apis nigrocincta
children.description: Native to the Philippines and Sulawesi. Builds single exposed combs. Very similar to Apis cerana but genetically distinct.

children.name: Bombus
children.description: Bumblebees — large, furry, social bees with annual colonies that collapse each autumn and restart from a single overwintered queen each spring. Their large bodies and the ability to 'buzz pollinate' (sonication — vibrating the flight muscles at exactly the right frequency to shake pollen from certain flower anthers) makes them uniquely effective pollinators of tomatoes, peppers, and other crops that honey bees cannot pollinate efficiently. Bumblebees have declined severely across Europe and North America due to habitat loss, pesticides, and the spread of pathogens from commercial colonies.

children.name: Bombus terrestris
children.description: Europe's largest and most abundant bumblebee. Widely reared commercially for greenhouse pollination. Queens emerge from hibernation as early as January in mild winters. Introduced to several countries for agriculture with significant ecological consequences.

children.name: Bombus lapidarius
children.description: Unmistakable — entirely black with a bright red-orange tail. Nests underground, often in old mouse holes. Queens are particularly large and impressive. A good indicator of diverse wildflower habitat.

children.name: Bombus pascuorum
children.description: The only all-ginger British bumblebee. Nests in tussocky grass and hedgerow bases, constructing a ball of moss and plant material. One of the latest-flying bumblebees, sometimes active into November. Highly sensitive to agricultural intensification.

children.name: Bombus distinguendus
children.description: Formerly widespread across northern Europe; now restricted in Britain to the machair grasslands of northwest Scotland and the Northern Isles. One of Britain's most threatened bumblebees, requiring clover-rich, unimproved grassland for survival.

children.name: Bombus hortorum
children.description: Has the longest tongue of any British bumblebee — up to 20 mm — enabling it to access deep tubular flowers such as foxglove, red clover, and honeysuckle that other bumblebees cannot reach.

children.name: Bombus ruderatus
children.description: One of the largest British bumblebees, with a very long tongue. Declined severely across Europe due to agricultural intensification. Introduced to New Zealand and Chile for clover pollination.

children.name: Bombus muscorum
children.description: Builds a nest of moss and grass above ground. Declining across Europe due to habitat loss. The orange-brown thorax and black abdomen with a white tail make it distinctive.

children.name: Bombus hypnorum
children.description: A recent colonist of Britain (first recorded 2001), now spreading rapidly. Nests in tree cavities and bird boxes. Characteristic ginger thorax and white tail.

children.name: Bombus pratorum
children.description: One of the first bumblebees to emerge in spring, sometimes as early as February. Small-sized with a golden-yellow collar and a white tail tinged with orange. Common in gardens and woodlands.

children.name: Bombus lucorum
children.description: A very common bumblebee of northern Europe and Asia. Forms a cryptic species complex with Bombus cryptarum and Bombus magnus. Colonies can contain up to 400 workers.

children.name: Bombus imbecillus
children.description: A high-altitude bumblebee of the Andes, found at elevations up to 4,500 metres in Ecuador and Colombia. One of the few South American bumblebees adapted to páramo grassland.

children.name: Bombus pauloensis
children.description: A widespread South American bumblebee ranging across Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay. Important pollinator of native Solanaceae species.

children.name: Bombus atratus
children.description: A large, entirely black bumblebee native to South America, from Colombia to Chile. Widely used commercially for pollination in greenhouse agriculture across the continent.

children.name: Bombus ignitus
children.description: A common bumblebee of Japan, Korea, and eastern China. Rearing techniques for this species have been developed for commercial use in greenhouse pollination.

children.name: Bombus crochetii
children.description: A southern European bumblebee, also known as the orange-tailed bumblebee for its characteristic bright orange abdomen tip. Favours dry, warm habitats.

children.name: Bombus sylvarum
children.description: One of Britain's rarest and most threatened bumblebees. Its high-pitched buzz (distinctly shrill) gives it its name. Requires extensive flower-rich grassland.

children.name: Bombus veteranus
children.description: A medium-sized bumblebee found across Europe and into Siberia. Declining in much of its range. Prefers wet meadows and coastal habitats.

children.name: Bombus rufocinctus
children.description: A North American bumblebee found across Canada and the northern United States. Highly variable colouration with a distinctive red belt between the wing bases.

children.name: Bombus nevadensis
children.description: A large western North American bumblebee with an exceptionally long tongue. Important pollinator of deep-tubed flowers such as penstemons and lupines.

children.name: Bombus pensylvanicus
children.description: Once one of the most common bumblebees of North America, now in severe decline across much of its range. Listed as Endangered by the IUCN. Important pollinator of alfalfa and sunflower.

children.name: Bombus affinis
children.description: The first bumblebee to be listed as Endangered in the United States. Once widespread across eastern North America, now reduced to a few isolated populations. Named for the rust-coloured patch on its abdomen.

children.name: Bombus terricola
children.description: A northern North American bumblebee in decline across much of its range. Parasitised by the cuckoo bumblebee Bombus insularis. Nests underground, often in abandoned rodent burrows.

children.name: Bombus bifarius
children.description: A common western North American bumblebee with two distinct colour forms — one red-banded, one black. Important in commercial greenhouse pollination, particularly for cranberries and blueberries.

children.name: Bombus transvaalensis
children.description: A species of Bombus found across Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Australia and Europe.

children.name: Bombus coccineus
children.description: A species of Bombus found across Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Australia and Europe.

children.name: Xylocopa
children.description: Carpenter bees — large, robust, mostly solitary bees that nest by excavating tunnels in dry, dead wood with their powerful mandibles. The Violet Carpenter Bee is the largest wild bee in Europe; females are jet black with iridescent violet-blue wings, while males are tawny brown with green eyes. Unlike bumblebees they do not form colonies — each female constructs her own nest, partitioning it into brood cells stocked with pollen. They are important pollinators of open flowers and buzz-pollinate in a manner similar to bumblebees.

children.name: Xylocopa violacea
children.description: Europe's largest bee, up to 28 mm. Females excavate up to 30 cm of tunnel in dead wood, stocking each cell with a pollen-and-nectar ball before sealing it. Expanding northward in Europe as temperatures rise; now breeding in southern England.

children.name: Xylocopa virginica
children.description: The most widespread carpenter bee in eastern North America. Males are harmless (no sting) but hover aggressively at eye level. Females can reuse the same nest tunnels for years. Important pollinator of tomatoes, eggplants, and passionfruit.

children.name: Xylocopa auripennis
children.description: A Southeast Asian carpenter bee with distinctive golden-banded abdominal hairs and metallic blue wings. Nests in dead bamboo and hardwood.

children.name: Xylocopa caerulea
children.description: A striking species with bright blue pubescence covering the thorax and a black abdomen. Found in Southeast Asia and India. Pollinates large tropical flowers including morning glories and orchids.

children.name: Xylocopa latipes
children.description: One of the largest carpenter bees, with a wingspan up to 50 mm. Native to South and Southeast Asia and northern Australia. The male has conspicuously enlarged hind legs.

children.name: Xylocopa micans
children.description: A small carpenter bee found in the southeastern United States and northern Mexico. Unlike its larger relatives, it sometimes nests colonially. Active year-round in warm climates.

children.name: Xylocopa californica
children.description: The largest bee in western North America. Nests in dead agave, yucca, and oak. Females are black with smoky wings; males are golden-brown. Important pollinator in desert and chaparral ecosystems.

children.name: Eulaema
children.description: Orchid bees (Euglossini) — metallic-coloured solitary bees of the Neotropics, with an extraordinary relationship with orchids. Male orchid bees collect fragrant compounds from orchid flowers — not for food but to construct complex chemical bouquets stored in specialised pouches in their hind legs. These scents are used in courtship display to attract females. In exchange, the orchid attaches its pollen mass (pollinium) to the bee's body; the bee carries it to the next orchid flower and effects pollination. Many orchid species are entirely dependent on a single species of orchid bee.

children.name: Eulaema meriana
children.description: One of the largest orchid bees, with a striking metallic golden-orange and black abdomen. Pollinates several orchid genera exclusively, including some bucket orchids (Coryanthes) that trap males to ensure pollinium attachment.

children.name: Eulaema nigrita
children.description: A large black orchid bee with dense hair, ranging from Mexico to Brazil. Unlike the metallic Euglossa, Eulaema species are black and resemble bumblebees. An important orchid pollinator.

children.name: Eulaema polychroma
children.description: A large, strikingly coloured orchid bee with golden-orange and dark banded pubescence. Found from Costa Rica to Bolivia. Males gather scent compounds from orchids of the genus Catasetum.

children.name: Melipona
children.description: Stingless bees of the Meliponini tribe — the most diverse group of eusocial bees, with over 500 species across the tropics. Unlike honey bees they do not have a functional sting; instead they defend their nests by biting and entangling intruders in sticky resin called propolis. Many Melipona species produce honey that is more acidic and slower to crystallise than Apis honey, with a distinctively complex flavour. Meliponiculture — the keeping of stingless bees — was practised throughout pre-Columbian Mesoamerica and remains a cultural tradition across Central and South America.

children.name: Melipona beecheii
children.description: Cultivated since pre-Columbian times by the Maya for its honey. Stingless bees of the genus Melipona are the 'melipona' bees kept in log hives called 'jobones'. Nearly wiped out across Central America by competition from Africanised honey bees.

children.name: Melipona quinquefasciata
children.description: A stingless bee native to Brazil, where it is known as 'uruçu'. Valued for its honey production and kept in 'meliponários'. Produces a distinctive honey with a tangy, acidic flavour.

children.name: Melipona subnitida
children.description: A stingless bee of the Brazilian Caatinga, a semi-arid region. Known for its heat tolerance and for producing a highly prized medicinal honey. Native peoples have kept it in artificial hives for centuries.

children.name: Melipona fasciata
children.description: A large stingless bee ranging from Mexico to northern South America. Known locally as 'virgencita'. Important pollinator in tropical forests and traditional Mayan beekeeping.

children.name: Tetragonisca
children.description: Tiny stingless bees of the Neotropics. Tetragonisca angustula, known as 'jataí', is one of the most widespread and best-studied stingless bee species. Colonies contain several thousand workers and are housed in tree cavities or human-made hives. Their honey is prized across Latin America for both its flavour and reputed medicinal qualities.

children.name: Tetragonisca angustula
children.description: A tiny stingless bee only 4 mm long, found from Mexico to Argentina. One of the most widespread neotropical stingless bees. Its honey is highly valued for medicinal purposes in many Latin American cultures.

children.name: Scaptotrigona
children.description: A genus of stingless bees found in Central and South America. Known for their strong colony defence — workers bite and cover intruders with resin, and some species also produce formic acid. Their honey is commercially harvested in parts of Brazil and Mexico.

children.name: Scaptotrigona postica
children.description: A stingless bee native to Brazil, known for its aggressive colony defence — it bites and entangles intruders in sticky resin rather than stinging. An important pollinator in Atlantic Forest ecosystems.

children.name: Euglossa
children.description: Orchid bees (Euglossini) — some of the most visually striking of all bees, with brilliant metallic colours in green, blue, gold, and purple. Males collect volatile compounds from orchid flowers and store them in specialised hind-leg pouches, using these scent cocktails in courtship displays. Each male's odour is unique and reflects his foraging history. More than 100 species are known.

children.name: Euglossa imperialis
children.description: A metallic green orchid bee ranging from Mexico to Brazil. Males collect aromatic compounds from orchids to attract females. The brilliant iridescent colouration makes it one of the most beautiful of all bees.

children.name: Euglossa viridissima
children.description: A brilliant metallic green orchid bee found from Mexico to Costa Rica. Males perform complex aerial displays to attract females, displaying their fragrance bouquets.

children.name: Euglossa annectans
children.description: A large orchid bee of the Colombian and Ecuadorian Andes. Its exceptionally long tongue, exceeding the length of its body, allows it to access the deepest orchid spurs.

children.name: Euglossa cognata
children.description: A deep blue-metallic orchid bee widespread across the Amazon basin. One of the most commonly encountered Euglossa species in lowland tropical forests.

children.name: Ceratina
children.description: Small carpenter bees — tiny, slender, mostly solitary bees that nest in the pith of hollow stems and twigs. Many species have metallic blue or green colouration. Unlike their larger Xylocopa relatives they do not excavate wood but instead use existing cavities, partitioning the tunnel with pith walls to create brood cells. Some species are facultatively social.

children.name: Ceratina cyanea
children.description: A small, metallic blue-green carpenter bee found across Europe, North Africa, and western Asia. Nests in hollow plant stems, creating linear brood cells separated by chewed pith partitions.

children.name: Ceratina stricta
children.description: One of the largest Ceratina species, native to East Asia. Females excavate nest tunnels in the pith of dead stems of Rubus and other brambles. Often found in bamboo forests.

children.name: Ceratina aurata
children.description: A small carpenter bee with a distinctive golden-green metallic sheen. Widespread in South and Southeast Asia. Nests in dry plant stems and shows facultative social behaviour in some populations.

children.name: Anthophora
children.description: Flower bees — medium to large solitary bees that are among the earliest to emerge in spring. Many species have fast, darting flight and hover in front of flowers like hummingbirds. They nest in burrows in soil, riverbanks, and old walls. The genus includes some of the longest-tongued bees relative to body size.

children.name: Anthophora plumipes
children.description: One of the earliest spring-flying solitary bees in Europe. Males are beige with distinctive feathery hairs on their middle legs. Females are entirely black. Nest in soil banks and old walls.

children.name: Anthophora retusa
children.description: A solitary bee that nests in sandy soil, constructing waterproof brood cells lined with a glandular secretion. Declining across northern Europe. A specialist on Fabaceae flowers.

children.name: Anthophora urbana
children.description: A common western North American solitary bee. Nests in large aggregations in compacted soil. Males establish territories and hover in place like hummingbirds. Visits a wide variety of flowers.

children.name: Anthophora edwardsii
children.description: A large solitary bee of western North America. The male has distinctive pale facial markings. Builds nests in cliff faces and steep soil banks. Active from early spring through summer.

children.name: Amegilla
children.description: Digger bees — colourful, fast-flying solitary bees that excavate burrows in sandy soils. Many species have brilliant iridescent bands of blue, green, or white. They are buzz pollinators and are increasingly recognised as important alternatives to honey bees for crop pollination. Males often establish territories at nest sites and defend them vigorously.

children.name: Amegilla quadrifasciata
children.description: A solitary bee with distinctive pale abdominal bands, found across the Mediterranean and into Central Asia. Nests in burrows excavated in sandy soil. Males sleep clinging to plant stems by their mandibles.

children.name: Amegilla cingulata
children.description: A striking Australian bee with iridescent blue bands across its black abdomen. Known for 'buzz pollination' — vibrating its flight muscles to release pollen from tomato, eggplant, and kiwi flowers. Nests in burrows in soft rock or mortar.

children.name: Nomada
children.description: Cuckoo bees — cleptoparasitic bees that lay their eggs in the nests of ground-nesting Andrena mining bees. The adult Nomada enters a host nest and deposits an egg; the Nomada larva consumes the host's pollen ball and starves the host larva. They are wasp-like in appearance, brightly marked in yellow, red, and black, with little or no body hair.

children.name: Nomada lathburiana
children.description: A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee that lays its eggs in the nests of the spring-flying Andrena mining bees. The female enters a host nest while the host is away and deposits an egg in the brood cell. Wasp-like in appearance.

children.name: Nomada goodeniana
children.description: A common cuckoo bee in Europe, parasitising the nests of mining bees in the genus Andrena. The female nomada bee lacks pollen-carrying structures and never collects pollen for her young.

children.name: Nomada flava
children.description: A yellow-and-black patterned cuckoo bee that parasitises Andrena flavipes and related mining bees. Often seen flying low over bare ground searching for host nest entrances.
